title: Neural Architecture
use_for: attention, embedding, normalization, and feed-forward layer boundaries
mapping model topology onto explicit tensor runtime contracts
do_not_use_for: generic ML framework usage without custom runtime design
rules: define tensor contracts before layer responsibilities
separate layer topology from tensor ownership
compile repeated forward paths into explicit command or stage sequences where possible
avoid framework assumptions and reflection-heavy graph construction
procedure: Define semantic inputs, outputs, and tensor axis meanings.
Map each layer's inputs, outputs, parameters, and scratch tensors to runtime tensor contracts.
Define layer graph and deterministic forward order.
Classify each operation as command-stream, CPU-side semantic transform, or unsupported prototype path.
Expose inference entrypoint and required synchronization boundaries.
Separate training hooks without letting them alter forward ownership.

primary_when: request is about neural network design, layers, forward pass, attention, transformer, embedding, or inference topology
request asks how model structure should map to the custom tensor runtime
must_not_own_when: request is about UI, patching, loader systems, or non-neural GPU rendering

solves: how to shape model topology around explicit tensor ownership
how to keep forward execution reviewable and deterministic
how to separate CPU-side prototype layers from backend-executable runtime layers
does_not_solve: choosing a third-party ML framework as the foundation
GPU buffer lifecycle without neural model context

core_principles: Layer architecture is secondary to tensor contracts in this domain.
Every layer must name its inputs, outputs, parameters, gradients if any, scratch tensors, and axis interpretation.
Forward execution should be a stable ordered product, not an emergent object traversal.
Prototype CPU tensor layers are allowed only when their boundary to the runtime tensor model is explicit.
Attention and transformer structures should preserve batch, sequence, head, and feature semantics until a deliberate 3D projection is chosen.
constraints: Do not hide tensor allocations inside layer constructors without an arena policy.
Do not allow a layer to own parameter arrays outside the runtime ownership model unless it is marked prototype-only.
Do not let inference mutate training-only state.
Do not flatten sequence or head axes without recording how they are recovered by downstream operations.
Do not introduce dynamic reflection-based layer graphs for core execution.
decision_rules: Use tensor-runtime-architecture first when model design depends on tensor layout or memory ownership.
Use architecture as primary when the user asks about layer topology, attention, transformer flow, embedding, normalization, or forward graph.
Attach training when the same structure needs backward paths, losses, optimizers, or checkpoints.
Attach optimization when layer changes are motivated by throughput, stability, precision, or data movement.

preferred_patterns: layer contract table listing tensors, roles, shapes, and owner
compiled forward command arrays for stable dense or elementwise paths
explicit CPU prototype labels for transformer/attention code not yet backend-executable
separate parameter, activation, scratch, and output tensors
batch-size-aware resizing through the tensor runtime, not per-layer ad hoc arrays
avoid_patterns: layers constructing hidden temporary arrays in hot inference paths
forward graph traversal that changes based on mutable collection order
mixing inference tensors with optimizer state
semantic tensor reshapes that silently materialize or copy data
attention implementations that erase axis meaning before masking and projection rules are defined

recipes.name: Design a runtime-backed layer
recipes.when: Use when adding or reviewing a layer intended for the custom runtime.
steps: Define input, output, parameter, gradient, and scratch tensor roles.
Map each tensor into a 3D runtime shape.
Define forward commands or kernel stages and the order they execute.
Define whether batch-size changes resize, reuse, or reallocate each tensor.
Add diagnostics for output shape, arena usage, and unsupported fallback paths.

recipes.name: Classify a transformer component
recipes.when: Use when attention, embedding, normalization, or feed-forward code exists but may not be runtime-backed.
steps: Identify semantic axes: batch, sequence, model dimension, heads, head dimension, vocabulary, or mask.
Mark the component as runtime-backed, CPU prototype, or hybrid.
Define the 3D projection needed for backend execution.
List missing commands or kernels that prevent runtime execution.
Keep the CPU prototype isolated from training throughput assumptions.

pitfalls: Mistaking a correct CPU prototype for a backend-ready runtime layer.
Adding layer abstractions before tensor ownership is stable.
Treating batch-size changes as simple metadata while tensors need reallocation.
Letting hidden arrays bypass arena telemetry.
review_checklist: Does every layer have an explicit tensor contract?
Is the forward order deterministic and reviewable?
Are runtime-backed and prototype-only operations clearly separated?
Are batch, sequence, feature, head, and vocabulary axes preserved or deliberately projected?
Are allocations routed through the runtime ownership model?
Can inference run without touching training-only state?

references.title: Neural Tensor Runtime Architecture
references.interpretation: Use this before layer design when tensor layout or ownership is relevant.
references.why: Layer architecture depends on the runtime data model.

references.title: Neural Model Architecture
references.interpretation: Use this for forward graph, layer contract, and prototype/runtime boundary guidance.
references.why: It keeps model design aligned with explicit tensor runtime constraints.
